notes: Coverage is the last 7 days in Asia/Taipei.
Google-account-backed spreadsheet metadata was observed only through the in-app Browser because Google connectors are frozen for this run.
Items are deduplicated against the prior week by id and source URL; this run contains six new radar items.

highlights.title: Google-account source handling now needs visible browser proof
highlights.why: The June 26 connector freeze changes the operating risk more than the data itself: spreadsheet and Google-backed evidence must show account/context in the in-app Browser, or the run must stop instead of falling back to connectors.
highlights.next: Keep the browser-context line in future automation summaries: URL, visible account, visible file/context, and whether exact modified_time was available.

highlights.title: Agent work is moving from tool novelty to delegated work management
highlights.why: OpenAI published Codex economic-potential research the same week Codex Remote became generally available and CLI/IDE web search changed. The signal is that agents are becoming managed work surfaces, not just prompt boxes.
highlights.next: For every recurring Codex workflow, track delegated task scope, approval point, source mode, and handoff artifact.

highlights.title: InfoCenter rhythm now has an aggregate planning signal
highlights.why: The work-rhythm collector scanned 500 events and produced 480 medium-confidence person-level rhythm signals across 72 people without publishing raw event titles or comment bodies.
highlights.next: Use the rhythm view only for timing/check-in support; keep reimbursement sheets as the authoritative work/pay source.

items.title: MOHW social-assistance and social-work unit posted another fixed-term research role
items.oneLine: MOHW job listings show a Social Assistance and Social Work Department contract deputy researcher role posted on 2026-06-24 with a 2026-07-01 deadline.
facts: MOHW job information lists a contract deputy researcher role for the Social Assistance and Social Work Department published on 2026-06-24.
The same job list still shows the prior 2026-06-15 Social Assistance and Social Work Department role with a 2026-06-29 deadline.
yourTake: This is not a policy announcement, but it is a workforce-capacity signal inside the central social-work administration surface.
For weekly workforce radar, repeated temporary/replacement-role postings are worth tracking beside training-recognition windows because they affect implementation capacity.
actions: Add the 2026-07-01 deadline to the internal social-work capacity watch list.
If a partner discussion touches social-work policy delivery, mention staffing/capacity as an evidence category rather than only policy text.

credibility.reason: Primary MOHW job information page with unit and deadline.

followups: Recheck after 2026-07-01 for whether the role closes, extends, or repeats.

items.title: FSC expanded eNotice toward cash capital subscription-stock issuance notices
items.oneLine: FSC’s June 23 news list records an eNotice expansion that adds electronic notices for cash capital subscription-stock issuance.
facts: FSC news list shows a 2026-06-23 Securities and Futures Bureau item on expanding eNotice service scope.
The title says the expansion adds electronic notification for cash capital subscription-stock issuance notices.
The same FSC financial-education section remains updated during the week, keeping official consumer/education resources current.
yourTake: This is a financial-literacy operations signal: more investor notices are moving into electronic workflows, so consumer education needs to explain what is legitimate, what to check, and what not to ignore.
For FamilyFin-style education, the useful angle is a short anti-scam and document-reading checklist for ordinary investors.
actions: Draft a 30-minute outline: how to recognize an official securities eNotice, what action deadline matters, and when to call the institution.
Watch whether FSC publishes a consumer-facing FAQ or operational guide.

credibility.reason: Primary FSC news and education pages.

followups: Check FSC or TDCC pages for an eNotice FAQ before turning this into public copy.

items.title: Codex Remote reached GA and CLI/IDE local tasks gained web search behavior
items.oneLine: OpenAI release notes say Codex Remote is now generally available on all ChatGPT plans, while the Codex changelog adds web search behavior for local CLI/IDE tasks.
facts: ChatGPT release notes dated 2026-06-25 say Codex Remote is generally available on all ChatGPT plans.
The same note describes authenticated one-to-one QR pairing for Remote Control.
The Codex changelog dated 2026-06-25 says local tasks in Codex CLI and IDE Extension can use web search, with cached/live behavior controlled by configuration.
yourTake: This matters for Kevin’s automations because local work, browser/account evidence, and remote approval are now closer together operationally.
The risk is source-mode ambiguity: a run summary must say whether evidence came from live web, cached web, local files, or visible account browser.
actions: Add source-mode wording to recurring automation closeouts when web search or browser-backed evidence is used.
For account-connected runs, keep the Google connector freeze rule above any Codex web-search convenience.

credibility.reason: Primary OpenAI release notes and Codex changelog.

followups: Revisit automation summaries after the next remote-control run to confirm the source-mode wording is sufficient.

items.title: OpenAI published economic research on Codex and delegated agent work
items.oneLine: OpenAI’s June 25 research post frames Codex as evidence that capable agents enable longer, more complex, cross-functional work.
facts: OpenAI published “How agents are transforming work” on 2026-06-25.
The post describes an Economic Research paper measuring Codex’s economic potential at the frontier.
OpenAI says frontier users use capable agentic tools for longer, more complex, and more cross-functional work as the tools improve.
yourTake: The practical lesson is to design agent workflows around delegated task boundaries, not around single prompts.
For Kevin’s workbench/report automations, the useful metric is whether the agent produced a verified handoff artifact that a human can review quickly.
actions: Add one “delegated work unit” field to future workflow proposals: input, allowed tools, stop condition, proof artifact.
Use this source in governance discussions about why recurring agents need state, approval gates, and no-op behavior.

sources.label: OpenAI - How agents are transforming work

followups: Read the linked paper before converting this into a stronger internal workflow-design rule.

items.title: Claude reliability reports reinforce the need for provider fallback planning
items.oneLine: Public outage coverage around Claude elevated error rates is a reminder that agent workflows need graceful degraded modes and retry boundaries.
facts: Public reporting on 2026-06-24 described Anthropic investigating elevated Claude error rates after user disruption reports.
The issue is relevant to AI operations even when the exact incident details come from status/reporting rather than a product launch.
Kevin’s local workflows already separate verified, partially verified, fallback-only, and blocked states.
yourTake: This is a reliability/governance item, not a feature item.
The action is to keep degraded-mode labels visible rather than silently substituting another provider or connector.
actions: Add provider-availability and fallback labels to any AI workflow that depends on a single external model provider.
For Google-account-backed work, do not treat model/provider fallback as permission to bypass the browser-surface freeze.

credibility.reason: Operational status signal; useful for workflow reliability, not a product capability claim.

followups: Check the provider status page during any future blocked AI-provider run before blaming local scripts.

items.title: Discord’s June 25 update keeps social SDK and quest loops visible in product communication
items.oneLine: Discord’s blog index highlights a June 25 changelog alongside Social SDK and quest/reward content, continuing the pattern of social and reward loops as growth infrastructure.
facts: Discord’s blog page lists “Discord Update: June 25, 2026 Changelog.”
The same blog surface continues to promote Social SDK and quest/reward content as product-feature material.
The prior Nitro Rewards/Orbs item remains the strongest concrete reward-loop source in the current tracking set.
yourTake: The durable pattern is still small repeated participation loops rather than a full gamification system.
For FamilyFin/community use, the adaptation should start as one manual weekly challenge and proof-of-completion loop.
actions: Keep the reward-loop prototype tracking item open until one manual loop is sketched.
Do not build a platform feature until a manual weekly loop has evidence of repeat use.

credibility.reason: Primary company blog surface; useful for product-pattern tracking, not independent performance proof.

followups: Compare with Taiwan member-community behavior before any FamilyFin adaptation.
